blocks.text: Barbara Ward
Director of Operations
Garza, Leblanc and Porter
Re: Advising you on the strategy for your portfolio of businesses
Dear Ms. Ward:

This letter confirms that we will advise you on the strategy for the group's portfolio of businesses, and it sets down the terms of that work: what we will do, who will do it, what it costs, and what each of us takes on. I have written it plainly and kept it short where the subject allowed. You and I have talked this through more than once, so nothing in it should surprise you. If any term here differs from what we agreed, telephone me before you sign and we will correct it.
The Question We Are Answering

You run several operating businesses under one roof, built up over the years, and you want a clear answer to a question that is easy to state and hard to settle: which of them deserve more capital and management attention, which should hold their present course, which need work, and whether any no longer belong in the group. Our engagement builds toward that answer and a practical order for acting on it, and it proceeds along four lines.

items: A common read of each business on its own terms: what it earns, the capital it ties up, how fast it is growing, and how those figures compare from one unit to the next on a single definition.
The market each business competes in and its standing within that market, so that a unit's prospects are judged against its own industry rather than against the other members of your group.

A benchmark of each business against comparable companies outside the group, drawn from public reference points, so that a judgment of strong or weak carries an outside measure and not only an internal one.

A portfolio view that places every business on one map of attractiveness and competitive position, and from it a recommendation on where to invest, where to hold, where to intervene, and what a sensible sequence of moves would be.

blocks.text: Let me also mark what this work is not, so that nothing you expect is left unspoken. We do not value your businesses for sale, and we are not your investment bank; if a divestiture follows from the analysis, we set out the case for it and leave the transaction to advisors you engage for that purpose. We do not give legal or tax advice, and where a question turns on either we say so and send you to your counsel. We recommend where capital and attention should go, but the decision and its execution stay with your board and your managers. And we work from your businesses as they stand today; we do not audit their accounts, and we take the figures your people give us as the record.
How the Work Proceeds

We work from your own figures rather than from industry rules of thumb, and every number in a deliverable ties back to a workpaper we can produce on request. We keep what we find apart from what we recommend, and label each, because the two do not carry the same weight. And we bring you our thinking as it firms up instead of holding it for the end, so that nothing in the final material reaches you cold. Where a figure is still provisional, we mark it as provisional and say what would settle it.
Who Will Do It

Steven Hunt, Research Associate, leads the market and competitive read on each business, and he is the person to call on anything to do with how a unit is positioned in its industry. Melissa Casey, Research Associate, builds the outside benchmarks and the comparable-company references that measure each business against a standard beyond the group. I set the direction of the work and read everything before it reaches you. On your side, we will treat you, Barbara Ward, as our point of contact for data, access, and the decisions the work calls for, unless you name someone else to stand in that place.
Fee and Start Date

Our fee to {{fact:f:E-2019-002.client}} is {{fact:f:E-2019-002.fee}}, fixed for the scope set out above. We expect to begin on {{fact:f:E-2019-002.start}} and to reach a portfolio recommendation about nine months from that date. If you ask us to widen the scope, we will price the addition in writing and will not begin it until you have agreed that price. We prefer a fixed fee here so that your people can work with us without watching a clock.
Fees and Payment

We invoice the fixed fee in equal monthly installments across the term, each payable within thirty days of the invoice date. Out-of-pocket costs, chiefly travel to your sites and offices, are billed at cost and itemized on the invoice. We do not mark them up.
Term and Termination

The engagement runs from the start date above until we deliver the portfolio recommendation, unless one of us ends it sooner. Either party may terminate on fifteen days' written notice, for any reason or none. On termination you owe the fee earned to the effective date, prorated against the fixed fee, together with expenses already incurred, and we hand over our work in whatever state it has then reached.
Confidentiality

The performance and prospects of your individual businesses are among the most sensitive information the group holds, and we treat them accordingly. We will use what you give us only for this engagement, and disclose it outside our firm only as you direct in writing or as the law requires. We ask the same of you as to our methods, our workpapers, and the fee recorded in this letter.
Limitation of Liability

Our total liability arising out of this engagement, whether framed in contract, in tort, or on any other basis, is limited to the fees actually paid to us under it. Neither party will be liable to the other for indirect or consequential loss, including lost profits, however it arises. This limit does not reach our own fraud or willful misconduct. Our deliverables are prepared for your internal use; if you intend to place one before a lender, a buyer, or another third party who may rely on it, raise it with me first so that we can address the reliance in writing.
Governing Law

This letter is governed by the law of the state in which our principal office sits, without regard to its conflict-of-laws rules. Any dispute we cannot resolve between us will go to mediation before either party turns to the courts.

If these terms are acceptable, please countersign both copies of this letter and return one to me; the other is yours to keep. We are glad to take on this work for Garza, Leblanc and Porter, and Steven Hunt and Melissa Casey will be ready to begin on the date set out above.
Sincerely,
To: Melissa Casey
From: Steven Hunt
Subject: Getting the portfolio work underway for Garza, Leblanc and Porter

Melissa, before we get going on {{fact:f:E-2019-002.start}} I wanted the two of us reading from the same page, because a portfolio study is the kind of work that sprawls if you let it. {{fact:f:E-2019-002.client}} runs several businesses under one roof, and Barbara Ward has been plain about the question the board is really asking: where should the group put its money and its attention next, and is there anything in the mix that has stopped earning its place. Everything we do should point back at that one question. If we keep it in front of us, I think we can hold the work tight.
How I would carve up the work

items: The inside read. We pull each business's own numbers together, side by side, on a common definition, so that revenue, margin, the capital each ties up, and its growth line all mean the same thing across the group. Barbara Ward's operations team is our way in for most of this.

The market read, which is mine. For each business I want to know the market it sits in, how fast that market is moving, and where the unit stands against the people it competes with. My early hunch is that a couple of these units look stronger on the inside numbers than their markets will support over time, but that is exactly the sort of thing I would rather test than assert, so treat it as a flag and nothing more.

The outside benchmark, which is yours. You set each business against comparable companies we can read from outside, so that we have a measure of strong and weak that does not come only from within the group.

The synthesis. Late in the engagement we put every business on one map, attractiveness against position, and turn that into a view on where the money should go and in what order. That part waits until the first three lines are solid.

blocks.text: One note on how we handle their side. Barbara Ward mentioned that her operations people are already stretched, so let us consolidate every request, ask once, and keep the log tight enough that nobody at Garza, Leblanc and Porter is chased twice for the same file. A fair amount of the unit-level performance data is sensitive inside the group as well, so it stays between us and does not travel.
The first couple of weeks

items: I get one consolidated data request over to Barbara Ward this week, rather than dribbling asks at her team, and I open a log of what we have requested and what has come back.
You and I agree the common definitions for a business, its margin, and the capital it uses before either of us calculates anything, so that we are not arguing in month four about what a number means.
I start the market scan on the two largest units, since they will carry most of the answer.
We compare notes at the end of next week, gaps and all, and see whether the shape of the portfolio is starting to show.

blocks.text: None of this is a finding yet, obviously; it is a plan and a data request going out the door. Poke holes in any of it now, while changing it costs nothing. If the definitions or the split of work look off to you, say so before we are committed to them. And if the request is going to land badly with anyone on their side, better we hear it from you before it goes out than from Barbara Ward afterward.

To: Barbara Ward
Cc: Melissa Casey
Barbara,

Thank you for the operating figures your team sent through last week. They came in clean and well labeled, which makes our side of the work a good deal faster. Melissa Casey has begun lining the businesses up against outside comparables, and I have started the market read on the two largest units, so the first picture of the {{fact:f:E-2019-002.client}} portfolio is beginning to take shape. We are not drawing conclusions from it yet; there is more to fold in before any of it holds still.

A couple of things would help us keep pace. We are still short the segment-level margins for the smaller businesses, and without those we cannot put the units on a common footing that means much. And I would like to sit with your operations people for an hour on each of the two main units, to walk through how the business actually runs; could you point me to the right person for each? We will schedule ourselves around their week, not the reverse.

Nothing here needs a decision from you beyond those two items. I am keeping a log of every request so that no one on the Garza, Leblanc and Porter team is chased twice for the same file, and I will copy you as items close.
Best,
Steven Hunt
Minutes of the working session on the portfolio strategy for Garza, Leblanc and Porter, held at the client's offices. Recorded by Melissa Casey.
Attendees

items: Melissa Casey, Research Associate
Steven Hunt, Research Associate
Barbara Ward, Director of Operations, {{fact:f:E-2019-002.client}}

blocks.text: What we covered

The session reviewed where each of the three workstreams stands and settled a handful of open definitions. We are far enough in to place real numbers against each business, but not yet at the point of a portfolio recommendation, which I said at the start.

Steven Hunt walked through the inside read. The common financial picture is now built for the larger businesses and reconciles to the figures the operations team supplied; two of the smaller units are still being normalized, because their segment margins arrived late. He kept what is confirmed apart from what is still being cleaned, and nothing discussed today rests on the units that remain open.

On the market read, Steven Hunt reported that the two largest units sit in markets moving at very different speeds, and that one of them looks stronger on its internal numbers than its market is likely to support. He marked that as a working read rather than a conclusion, pending the competitor detail still to come in.

I presented the outside benchmark. Against comparable companies, the group's margins are middling in two businesses and clearly below reference in a third. I set out where each comparison is firm and where it rests on a thin set of references, and where the reference base is thin I said so rather than smoothing it over.

One figure still carries a caveat. The margin for the third business depends on an allocation of shared overhead that the group makes one way and that comparable companies make another; until we restate it on a like-for-like basis, the gap to the benchmark is stated as a range rather than a single number. Barbara Ward agreed to have her finance contact confirm how the overhead is currently allocated.

Barbara Ward's main question was whether the smaller units could be set aside for now so that the work concentrates on the businesses that carry most of the group's capital. We agreed that the smaller units stay in the baseline for completeness but will not hold up the portfolio map, and that her team would supply the outstanding segment margins together with a first list of competitors for the two large units.

We also touched on sequencing. With the larger businesses now measured and the two smaller units close behind, the natural next block of work is the portfolio map. Steven Hunt and I agreed to hold the map back until the competitor detail for the two large units is in, so that the position axis is not built on a partial view, and Barbara Ward had no objection to that order.
Action items

rows: Deliver the outstanding segment margins for the smaller businesses and confirm the overhead allocation
Normalize the two smaller units into the common financial picture
Firm up the outside benchmark for the third business and widen its reference base
Begin the competitive read on the two largest units
Set the next working session once the items above are in hand

blocks.text: Status Report: Portfolio Strategy

items: Client of record: {{fact:f:E-2019-002.client}}
Prepared for the operations sponsor at Garza, Leblanc and Porter
Prepared by Steven Hunt, with Melissa Casey
Reporting period: the four weeks ending with this report
Engagement roughly at its midpoint

blocks.text: Where things stand

The engagement reached its midpoint this period and is on plan. The inside read is complete for every business in the group, the outside benchmark is substantially done, and we have begun turning both into the portfolio view that the recommendation will rest on. Measured against the engagement budget of {{fact:f:E-2019-002.fee}}, we are a little under half spent, which tracks the work performed rather than the calendar; the heavier synthesis hours fall in the periods ahead, and the budget was built around that. Nothing we have found points to a change in the fee or the timeline.

The short version, for anyone reading only this far: the diagnostic work is essentially finished, the picture is close to what we expected going in, and the work now turns from measuring each business to weighing the businesses against one another. We are not recommending anything yet, and will not until the portfolio map is built and tested.
Completed this period

items: Finished the common financial read across every business, including the two smaller units whose segment margins came in late and are now reconciled.
Completed the market read on the two largest units and confirmed that they sit in markets moving at very different speeds.
Brought the outside benchmark to substantial completion; Melissa Casey has each business set against a comparable set, with the reference base for the weakest unit now widened enough to rely on.
Restated the third business's margin on a like-for-like overhead basis, which closed the range we were carrying on it at the last session.
Held two working sessions on the largest units and documented how each business actually runs, which grounds the market read in more than the numbers.
Reconciled the unit-level figures to the group's own management accounts with your finance team, so that our baseline matches the numbers your reporting produces.

blocks.text: None of that has gone to your board as a recommendation. These results are inputs to the portfolio view, and we have labeled them that way on every internal exhibit, so that no one mistakes a measurement for a verdict on a business.

A word on how the two sides have worked this period, since it bears on the pace. Your finance team turned the reconciliation questions around quickly, which is the main reason the inside read closed on schedule, and Melissa Casey and I have kept the single request log current so that nothing is asked for twice. The bulk of our hours this period went into cleaning and reconciling data rather than into analysis; that is the ordinary shape of a study at this stage, and it is why the lighter-looking spend does not mean the work is behind.
The period ahead

items: We place every business on one map of market attractiveness and competitive position, so that the group can be seen whole rather than one unit at a time.
Melissa Casey drafts the benchmark exhibits so that each business reads on a single page against its comparables.
We test each unit's standing against a normal year rather than the last twelve months alone, so that one soft or strong quarter does not tilt where a business lands on the map.
We walk the draft portfolio map past you before the next formal session, so that the options arrive already framed against how you see the businesses.
We prepare a first, clearly labeled set of portfolio options for the next working session, rather than a single answer.

blocks.text: Risks and what we are watching

items: Competitor detail. The market read on the two large units leans on competitor information that is still partly incomplete; if the remaining detail shifts the picture, it will move where those units land, and you will hear it from us before it changes any recommendation.

Thin references on the smaller units. The comparable set for the smallest businesses is narrow, and a narrow benchmark can flatter or punish a unit unfairly; we are widening it and will flag any conclusion that rests on too few references.

Questions at the edge of scope. A couple of your managers have raised questions about pricing inside individual units that sit just outside the portfolio scope. We have logged them and will flag any that would change the fee before doing the work, not after.

blocks.text: On balance, the work sits where it should at the halfway mark, and the coming period turns the diagnosis into portfolio options you can weigh. If the next period runs to plan, those options will be in your hands with room to consider them before the final month. I will hold this report to the same monthly cadence, and the request log stays open for anyone at {{fact:f:E-2019-002.client}} who wants to see what is outstanding.
Notes from the working session on the portfolio strategy for Garza, Leblanc and Porter, at the client's offices. Recorded by Melissa Casey.
Present

items: Melissa Casey, Research Associate
Steven Hunt, Research Associate
Barbara Ward, Director of Operations, {{fact:f:E-2019-002.client}}

blocks.text: Discussion

This session was about choosing among the portfolio options, not building them. The map of the businesses has held up through testing, and the purpose today was to put the choices in front of Barbara Ward and walk the trade-offs before we commit to a single recommendation.

Steven Hunt presented the portfolio map. Two businesses fall in the invest quadrant, with a strong position in markets that are still growing; two sit in hold, sound enough but in markets that are flat; and one lands in the fix-or-exit corner, a weak position in a market that is not helping it. He noted that the placement does not change when we set aside the last twelve months for a more normal year, which gives us some confidence the map is not resting on an unusual period.

We spent most of the hour on the two hold businesses, because they are the ones where reasonable people could disagree. Both earn their keep today, but one competes in a market that comparable companies are quietly leaving, and the question is whether hold there really means hold and watch. Steven Hunt's view was that it does, provided the group sets a marker now for what would move that unit into the fix-or-exit column later. I noted where the outside references support that reading and where they are still too thin to lean on.

I confirmed that every business on the map traces to both an inside figure and an outside comparable, and that the reference base for the smallest unit, thin at the last session, is now wide enough to rely on. There are no open benchmark items on our side.

On the fix-or-exit unit, I laid out the outside comparables once more, so that the group could see the judgment is not ours alone. Comparable companies in that market carry both thinner margins and slower growth than the group's other businesses, and several have narrowed or left the line over the last few years. That is the reference base behind placing the unit in that corner, and I marked the two references I would treat as the weakest of the set.

Barbara Ward's read was that the board will move on the two invest businesses without much argument, but will not accept a recommendation to exit the weakest unit without a clearer account of what leaving it in place would cost the group over the next few years. She asked us to develop that before we recommend, and was comfortable treating the two hold businesses as steady for now.

Before we closed, we settled the shape of the final readout. Barbara Ward preferred a short recommendation for the board, with the full portfolio map and the benchmark detail behind it as an appendix, so that the reasoning is on hand without crowding the decision. We will build the final material to that shape.
Actions

items: Steven Hunt: cost the case for leaving the weakest unit in place over the next few years, as the counterweight to an exit recommendation. Before the final session.
Melissa Casey: assemble the final exhibit pack and confirm every business ties to both an inside figure and an outside comparable. Before the final session.
Steven Hunt: firm up the recommendation on the two invest businesses so that it can be put to the board at the right level. Before the final session.
Barbara Ward: confirm the board's appetite for an exit so that the recommendation on the weakest unit is pitched realistically. Within two weeks.
